title: Cookie Chain — Introduction
content:
Cookie Chain — Introduction

Cookie Chain is a community-run Solana fork. It started as a Solana fork. The original group of community devs built a thriving chain and ecosystem. When the core team's vision stopped aligning with the community, the community forked the fork and kept shipping.

Today, Cookie Chain is operated by an expanding multi-validator set with COOK as the native asset. Real validators. Real governance. Real shipping.

What makes Cookie Chain different:
- Solana compatible: SPL tokens, programs, and tooling work out of the box.
- Community operated: no foundation gate, no single operator.
- Multi-sig bridge: COOK moves between Solana and Cookie Chain through an m-of-n community multi-sig — no single key controls the vault.
- Sub-second finality: ~1s block times with Solana-style consensus.
- Minimal fees: fees paid in COOK, priced for builders and users, not extractors.
- Dirt-cheap program deploys: deploying a program on Cookie Chain costs next to nothing compared to Solana mainnet.

title: Cookie Chain — Architecture & Consensus
content:
Cookie Chain — Architecture & Consensus

Cookie Chain inherits SVM architecture with PoH, Tower BFT, Turbine, Gulf Stream, Sealevel, and pipelining.

Proof of History (PoH): A verifiable delay function acts as a cryptographic clock and orders events before consensus runs.

Tower BFT: A PoH-optimized PBFT variant lets validators vote on the PoH sequence, reducing messaging overhead and supporting sub-second finality.

Turbine: block propagation via randomized shredding.
Gulf Stream: mempool-less transaction forwarding directly to the upcoming leader.
Sealevel: parallel program execution across CPU cores.
Pipelining: staged transaction validation for steady throughput.

Block time & finality:
- Target block time: ~80-140 ms
- Finality is sub-second under normal conditions.
- Consensus is produced by an expanding set of independent validators.

title: Cookie Chain — Validators
content:
Cookie Chain — Validators

Cookie Chain is operated by an expanding multi-validator set. Validator onboarding is open to qualified community members.

Validators secure the chain through stake and produce blocks using Solana-style consensus (Tower BFT + PoH). No single validator or foundation controls the chain.

View the current validator set at: cookiescan.io/validators

Validator incentives are funded through pump.fun creator fees from the Solana-side token launch, routed back into the ecosystem.

title: Cookie Chain — Community Multi-sig Bridge
content:
Cookie Chain — Community Multi-sig Bridge

COOK moves between Solana and Cookie Chain through a community-operated multi-sig. It is a lock/unlock bridge — no single party can move bridge funds unilaterally.

Flow: Solana to Cookie Chain:
- Lock on Solana: deposit COOK into bridge vault on Solana mainnet.
- Unlock on Cookie Chain: once the threshold is met, the multi-sig authorizes the corresponding asset to unlock on Cookie Chain.

Bridge vault address: G3mm95M4ns7mk8oseWGJnirvgyMahMz3vZEUhdJn8oGX (Vault 0)
Bridge frontend: bridge.cookiescan.io
Multisig transparency dashboard: sig.cookiechain.wtf/community

Token roles:
- sCOOK: Solana-side COOK (created on pump.fun)
- cCOOK: Cookie Chain-side COOK (the native COOK)
- Locked COOK: acquired locked allocation representing 27% of the supply

The plan: create pump.fun token on Solana (sCOOK), acquire the locked 27% COOK allocation, unlock bridge-out for locked holders, then operate the 1:1 sCOOK ↔ cCOOK relayer bridge under community multisig custody.

title: Cookiequad Multisig
content:
Cookiequad Multisig
Cookiequad Multisig
Creating teams, managing members and handling proposals on-chain.
Create your team, set permissions, and manage vaults with an intuitive interface.
0

on
COOKIE
CHAIN
Create Multisig
Manage Existing Multisig
Community Wallet
How It Works
Understand the multisig flow
1. Create a multisig with members, permissions, threshold, and optional authorities.
2. Fund a vault PDA and create proposals for transfers or configuration changes.
3. Members approve or reject proposals based on their voting permission.
4. Once a proposal is approved and any time lock has passed, an executor can run it.
What You Can Do
Core capabilities included
Create autonomous or controlled multisigs.
View members, permissions, vault balances, spending limits, and proposals.
Create vault transfer proposals and config proposals.
Approve, reject, cancel, activate, and execute proposals.
Use existing spending limits directly from the UI.

title: Cookie Chain — Break Gorbagana (Network Stress Test)
content:
Cookie Chain — Break Gorbagana (Network Stress Test)

An interactive stress-test tool on cookiescan.io that lets users hammer the Cookie Chain network with rapid RPC requests to test its performance.

Cookie Chain is a high-performance blockchain with ~1-second block times, sub-second finality, and low fees. The stress test demonstrates the network's capacity for high-throughput transactions.

URL: https://cookiescan.io/break-gorbagana

Powered by the Solana Virtual Machine (SVM) with proof-of-history consensus and parallel transaction processing.

title: Gorbagana Cash — Bridge
content:
Move Assets
Bridge
Bridge from Solana into Gorbagana Cash before interacting with the ecosystem.
Docs
Bridge
Bridge from Solana into Gorbagana Cash before interacting with the ecosystem.
The bridge is the entry path for users and builders coming from Solana. Move assets into GCASH, then use them for transactions, deployments, and dApps.
The bridge UI is being prepared for the community fork launch. Until then, bridge-related links and details are placeholders.
